coredata.dc:title: Enhanced antibacterial properties of the bracket under natural light via decoration with ZnO/carbon quantum dots composite coating
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jingxiang
An, Xiaoli
Li, Xiaoan
Liao, Xiaozhu
Nie, Yingying
Fan, Zengjie
coredata.dc:description: Enamel demineralization of the brackets caused by surrounding bacteria is the main challenge facing orthodontists. Aiming to resolve this issue, here we originally reported a novel antibacterial coating based on ZnO and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) composite prepared via a facile and environmentally-friendly method. As desired, the bracket modified with ZnO/CQDs coating exhibited excellent antibacterial performance than the unmodified bracket (Streptococcus mutans 96.13%, Staphylococcus aureus 90.28% and Escherichia coli 92.35%) under natural light. In addition, the natural light antibacterial mechanism was explored, which could be ascribed to the upconversion fluorescence of the CQDs and the interaction between CQDs and ZnO.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Upconversion fluorescence
Natural light
Antimicrobial performance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of ZnO/CQDs coating on the brackets

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The photograph of the bracket. (b) SEM image of the bracket. (c) The photograph of bracket modified with ZnO/CQDs coating. (d) SEM image of the bracket modified with ZnO/CQDs coating.
XRD pattern of the bracket unmodified and modified with ZnO/CQDs coating.
FTIR spectra of the bracket modified with ZnO coating and ZnO/CQDs coating.

Colony count and antibacterial rate. (a) The colony count of E. coli. (b) The colony count of S. mutans. (c) The colony count of S. aureus (d) Antibacterial rate of brackets modified with ZnO coating and ZnO/CQDs coating (*P < 0.01).
UV–Vis absorption spectra of the brackets modified with the ZnO coating and ZnO/CQDs coating.

(a) Upconversion PL spectra of CQDs. (b) Illustration for upconversion, electronic transport and photocatalytic antibacterial performance of brackets modified with ZnO/CQDs coating under natural light.

head.ce:title: Enhanced antibacterial properties of the bracket under natural light via decoration with ZnO/carbon quantum dots composite coating

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Stomatology, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Sensing Technology, Gansu Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The first two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Enamel demineralization of the brackets caused by surrounding bacteria is the main challenge facing orthodontists. Aiming to resolve this issue, here we originally reported a novel antibacterial coating based on ZnO and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) composite prepared via a facile and environmentally-friendly method. As desired, the bracket modified with ZnO/CQDs coating exhibited excellent antibacterial performance than the unmodified bracket (Streptococcus mutans 96.13%, Staphylococcus aureus 90.28% and Escherichia coli 92.35%) under natural light. In addition, the natural light antibacterial mechanism was explored, which could be ascribed to the upconversion fluorescence of the CQDs and the interaction between CQDs and ZnO.

ce:section.ce:para: As a common oral disease, the malocclusion has troubled lots of patients for a long time. The fixed orthodontic technique is considered as the most effective treatment in clinical practice. However, the enamel demineralization of the brackets have troubled the patients invariably and been reported by lots of literature, e.g., Tufekci et al., who reported that 14 cases of enamel demineralization occurred among 37 cases of fixed orthodontic treatment in 6 months (the prevalence ratio reached 38%). Further, 16 cases of enamel demineralization were observed after the 35 fixed orthodontic treatment in 12 months (the prevalence was about 46%) [1]. What’s more, Sudhakar’s group, who also reported that the prevalence rate of fixed appliance demineralization is nearly 68.4% [2]. As a consensus, the organic acid, produced by the Streptococcus mutans, is considered as the main reason for the demineralization [3,4]. Especially, most of the surface alterations caused by bonded devices would make it difficult to clean the tooth surface and maintain the oral hygiene around the brackets [5]. Moreover, the complex shapes of the bracket would provide plenty of attachment sites for the pathogens, which would decrease oral self-cleaning ability, resulting in the increased number of Streptococcus mutans [6,7]. In addition, the bracket material, surface treatment and other factors are also related to the absorption of the Streptococcus mutans [8–10]. Therefore, how to improve the antibacterial properties of the brackets would be the focus for the fixed orthodontic technique in future.

As known, lots of sterilization methods are presented, including uperization, ultraviolet sterilization, and disinfectant sterilization etc., while these methods could be hardly used for the special oral environment. Recently, the nano-semiconductor materials, with the unique photo-induced electron-hole pairs, have been reported as the hot issue in the photocatalytic field, such as ZnO, TiO2, WS2 and ZnS, et al. [11–14], which could also prove us a new horizon to conquer the issues of oral sterilization. Based on the energy band theory, when the light (the energy is greater than the band gap) irradiates on the surface of the semiconductor materials, the electrons (e−) on valence band would excite to the conduction band and leave a hole (h+) in the valence band. Then, most of those photo-induced electron-hole pairs would recombine rapidly, while part of those would react with the substances which adsorbed on the surface of catalysis, such as H2O, O2, OH−, to generate superoxide anion free radical (O2−), hydroxyl radical (HO−), or HO2− free radical [15]. Those free radicals with strong oxidizing property could completely decompose organic compounds into water and carbon dioxide, and at the same time, could also sterilize effectively.

With the being environmentally-friendly, highly biocompatible, and low-cost, the ZnO is considered as one of the most potential materials for this issue, which already has been applied in the photocatalytic field. E.g., Jiang et al., synthesized a visible-light-driven mesoporous graphitic carbon nitride/ZnO nanocomposite and found that the composite could significantly enhance its photocatalytic ability upon the degradation of methyl orange [16]. Tsang et al., prepared a three-dimensional (3D) reduced graphene oxide/WS2 nanosheet/Mg-doped ZnO nanohybrid and verified that the nanohybrid could completely decompose Rhodamine B within 5 min, meanwhile, its better antibacterial ability in dark was demonstrated [17]. More importantly, the transparent property could hardly influence on the visual effect when the ZnO covers on the surface of the bracket, which is another important advantage. As a pity, the ZnO with a band gap of 3.2 eV could only utilize the light in the ultraviolet and near ultraviolet region, which restricts its antibacterial application, especially in oral cavity sterilization owing to the carcinogenicity of ultraviolet to normal oral tissues.

In the recent decade, a novel carbon material, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), with its low-cost, environmentally-friendly, strongly fluorescent and highly biocompatible, has attracted lots of researchers’ interests, and has been widely applied in solar cells [18], cell imaging [19,20] or photocatalytic fields [21–27]. Especially with its upconversion fluorescence property, the CQDs could convert the low-frequency waves into high-frequency waves, which is a unique advantage compared with other materials. Until now, lots of researches on the upconversion fluorescence have been reported, especially in photocatalytic fields, because the CQDs could convert visible light into UV or near-UV light. It means that most of wide bandgap semiconductors could perform photocatalysis in the visible light, which could increase the visible light availability factor observably. For instance, Liu et al., who prepared the CQDs/TiO2 to improve the visible light photocatalysis [21]. Pan et al., who obtained the CQDs modification to achieve the visible light photocatalysis of the SnO2 [22]. Zhang’s group, who prepared the ZnO/CQDs nano-composite to improve the photocatalytic efficiency and the degradation rate of RhB could reach 98% [23] and so on. All above would provide us a new insight into designing the high antimicrobial brackets, which are composed of the photoelectric semiconductor and upconversion CQDs to improve the visible light bactericidal performance in oral environment, meanwhile there are few reports in literature as well.

In this paper, we prepared antibacterial bracket coated with the CQDs and ZnO dual layers via simple hydrothermal and sputtering methods. The results showed that the CQDs layer formed on the surface of the bracket was covered with the ZnO layer hermetically and exhibited an excellent natural light bactericidal performance against Streptococcus mutans (denoted as S. muans), Staphylococcus aureus (denoted as S. aureus) and Escherichia coli (denoted as E. coli). Furthermore, the mechanism of the enhancement of the bactericidal performance under natural light would be discussed.

Firstly, 0.75 g of sucrose was dispersed in 30 mL of deionized water and magnetically stirred for 30 min. The mixture was transferred into a 40 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 5 h, then the reacted solution was cooled to room temperature. Secondly, the CQDs were obtained by subsequently removing the mixed suspension, filtering and centrifuging processes. According to the previous study [24], the CQDs were diluted to the optimal concentration 1.5 mg/mL for further coating preparation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ZnO/CQDs coating on the brackets
ce:section.ce:para: The ordinary stainless steel brackets were chosen (0.022 type, Hangzhou Three Stars Than Dental Instrument Co., LTD, Hangzhou, China) for this experiment. The brackets were ultrasonically cleaned with deionized water for 30 min, and then the prepared CQDs solution was dropped on the surface of the brackets and dried at 80 °C.

After the formation of CQDs coating, the ZnO layers were prepared on the surface of brackets (The purity of target material for Chengdu Ultrapure Company Production 99.99% zinc oxide) by JZCK-580 high vacuum magnetron sputtering coating equipment (China Shenyang Science and Technology Development Co., Ltd). In detail, the background vacuum was 3.5 × 10−4 Pa, the working pressure was 1.0 Pa, the sputtering environment was protected by argon, the sputtering power was 150 W, the substrate temperature was 300 °C, the argon gas flow was 40 SCCM, and the sputtering time was 1 h. According to our previous study [25], the thickness of the prepared ZnO film is around 350 nm. The as-prepared samples were annealed at 350 °C at the rate of 5 °C/min for 1 h in nitrogen.

Three bacterial suspensions with the concentration of 1.0 × 106 CFU/mL, including common pathogens E. coli (ATCC35218) and S. aureus (ATCC25923), and oral common pathogens S. mutans (ATCC700610) were prepared. Then, 3 mL of liquid medium and 100 μL of each suspension were mixed together in a test tube. After that, the brackets (control group), the brackets modified with ZnO and the brackets modified with ZnO/CQDs coating (both as experiment groups) were placed in the prepared test tubes containing bacterial suspensions and incubated at 25 °C for 24 h under natural light. Each group was repeated for 5 times. The composition of the liquid medium was as follows: S. mutans were cultured on TPY medium containing tryptone, yeast extract, glucose, KH2PO4, K2HPO4, Na2CO3 and agar. E. coli and S. aureus were cultured in MH medium containing beef meal, soluble starch, and acidic hydrolyzed casein.

After incubation, the suspension was vigorously shaken and then 10 μL of each suspension was diluted in 10 mL of sterile saline. Then, 10 μL of the dilution was added into the cooled (55 °C) solid medium, shaken vigorously, and poured into a petri dish. After being cultured at 37 °C for 24 h, the number of bacterial colonies on each dish was recorded. Replicate this procedure three times. The antimicrobial activity was calculated using the surviving colony forming unit counts (CFU), and the antibacterial rate was calculated according to the following formula:
Antibacterial
rate
( % )
=
Control
group
-
Experiment
group
Control
group
× 100 %

The morphology of the samples was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM; Hitachi S-4800, Japan). The structure was characterized by the X-ray diffraction (Rigaku D/MAX-2400). The UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured by a deuterium lamp TU-1901 dual-beam UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The PL spectra were measured by a xenon lamp in aqueous solution at room temperature using a FLs920 steady state/transient spectrophotometer. The Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were measured by a Model 670 spectrometer from Thermo Nicolet Nexus FTIR.

The morphologies of the brackets unmodified and modified with ZnO/CQDs coating are shown in Fig. 1 . Comparison of visual inspection between unmodified and modified coating, we can find that the bracket modified with ZnO/CQDs coating presents a colorful surface which could be ascribed to the interference phenomenon of the ZnO coating (Fig. 1a and c). Although the ZnO/CQDs coating is introduced into the bracket, the transparent property of the composite coating can be observed, ensuring the texture and aesthetics of the bracket. In addition, the surface morphology is confirmed by the SEM (Fig. 1b and d). Before modification, the surface of the bracket is smooth. In contrast, after modification, the surface of the bracket presents a relatively coarser morphology, covered by a uniform and dense ZnO coating, which is beneficial for protecting the bracket.

Fig. 2 is the XRD pattern of the bracket unmodified and modified with ZnO/CQDs coating. Compared with the unmodified bracket, the bracket modified with ZnO/CQDs coating exhibits an obvious diffraction peak located at 34.4°, which corresponds to the (0 0 2) typical wurtzite structure zinc oxide (PDF36-1451) [25,26], indicating that the bracket are covered with highly crystalline ZnO. In addition, the characteristic diffraction peaks of CQDs were not found in the XRD pattern, the reason may be attributed to the small amounts and low crystallinity of the CQDs.

Fig. 3 shows the FTIR spectra of the different brackets (all the powders were scraped from the surface of the bracket, including ZnO powders and ZnO/CQDs powders). As shown, the bands at 3428 and 1628 cm−1 appearing in both samples could be attributed to the water adsorbed onto the surface, and the band at 1384 cm−1 appearing in ZnO/CQDs coating diminutively could be attributed to C-O vibrations of CQDs, which indicates that the CQDs layer has been introduced into the bracket [28]. The results of SEM, XRD, and FTIR prove that the composite coating is composed of CQDs and ZnO.

The number of surviving bacteria in the culture dish is used to evaluate the antibacterial property of the brackets unmodified and modified with ZnO coating and ZnO/CQDs coating. As shown in Fig. 4 a–c, the CFU of the E. coli, S. mutans and S. aureus cultured on the brackets modified with ZnO coating and ZnO/CQDs coating show a decreasing trend compared with the bracket without modification. In three groups, the bracket modified with the ZnO/CQDs has the least CFU. Meanwhile, the antibacterial rate of the brackets unmodified and modified with the ZnO coating and ZnO/CQDs coating is analyzed and shown in Fig. 4d. The antibacterial rate of the brackets modified with ZnO coating against E. coli, S. mutans and S. aureus is 57.14%, 45.31%, and 42.4% respectively. After the introduction of the CQDs, a high antibacterial rate against the above three bacteria can be observed, which is 92.35%, 96.13% and 90.28% respectively. The chi-square test proves that the antibacterial rate of the six groups is statistically significant (p < 0.01). All above results evidence that the introduction of CQDs could improve the natural light antibacterial performance effectively.

Fig. 5 is the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the different samples, which show that the absorption of brackets modified with ZnO/CQDs coating exhibit an obvious enhancement in the visible light region. The increasing absorption could be ascribed to the introduction of CQDs and considered as one of the main reasons for the enhancement of visible light photocatalytic antibacterial activity [23].

Fig. 6 a is the upconversion PL spectra of the CQDs measured by the excitation wavelengths ranging from 550 to 850 nm. It is obvious that the up-converted emission is located in the range of 380 nm and exhibits an excitation-dependent behavior. Fig. 6b is an illustration for revealing the antibacterial mechanism of the bracket modified with ZnO/CQDs coating. In this system, with the bandgap of 3.2 eV, the ZnO films on the surface can utilize part of ultraviolet light in natural light directly. After CQDs are introduced into the composites system, a mass of visible light is converted to ultraviolet and near ultraviolet light, which is greater than the bandgap of ZnO and could increase the photo-generated electron-hole pairs to enhance the photocatalytic germicidal activity. What’s more, CQDs could accept the photo-generated electrons from ZnO to promote the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs and significantly increase the photoelectric conversion response of the bracket system, which is another important reason for the enhancement of the photocatalytic germicidal activity [29], and the similar results have been reported by Liu et al. [30] and Du et al. [31].

Thus, the bracket modified with ZnO/CQDs coating has remarkable visible light germicidal activity, which would be considered as an environmental and self-cleaning medical device applied in future oral medicine field.

The ZnO/CQDs coating covered the bracket was successfully prepared via a simple method and proved to exhibit good antibacterial activity under natural light. The main reason could be attributed to the unique conversion photoluminescence of CQDs. In addition, the CQDs could promote the separation of electron-hole pairs to increase the photoelectric conversion response and the ZnO (3.2 eV) could utilize part of ultraviolet light are also considered as important reasons. Such simple, harmless, low-cost and high germicidal ZnO/CQDs coating is expected to get a wide application on surface treatment of various oral medical devices for orthodontic treatment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We deeply appreciate the support from Natural Science Foundation of China (81571829), the Medical Subject Fund of Stomatology College of Lanzhou University (201502-3), and the open project of State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (LSL-1505), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (lzujbky-2017-145, lzujbky-2017-it47).
